coredata.dc:title: A highly permeable nanoplatform based on functionalized carbon dots for synergistic reactive oxygen/nitrogen species tumor therapy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiangyong
Li, Qin
Yang, Qian
Tang, Qi
Hu, Xiaoyi
Liu, Qing
Zhang, Liangke
coredata.dc:description: Reactive oxygen species (ROS)-based antitumor strategies, particularly chemodynamic therapy, have garnered considerable attention. However, challenges such as difficulties in achieving deep penetration, relatively low H2O2 levels in the tumor microenvironment, the requirement for low pH by the Fenton reaction, and their short lifespan have impeded satisfactory therapeutic outcomes. Hence, we have developed a nanoplatform with enhanced permeability that not only generates significant amounts of ROS but also converts them into longer-lasting reactive nitrogen species (RNS), thereby improving tumor therapy efficacy. In our study, carbon dots were functionalized by doping with gold atoms and grafting nitrosoglutathione (GSNO) to form ACN, which exhibits glucose oxidase-like properties and enables laser-responsive NO release. ACN and indocyanine green (ICG) were then loaded onto MnO2 nanoflowers to form MnO2@AI. Upon arrival at the tumor site, MnO2 reacts with H2O2 and GSH, leading to its degradation and the subsequent release of ACN, which is characterized by three permeation-promoting properties: ultra-small size, positive charge, and NO content. In addition, ACN promotes H2O2 production through glucose metabolism and reduces pH, both of which enhance the Fenton-like reaction of MnO2, thereby amplifying ROS generation. The ICG in MnO2@AI enhances its photothermal properties, leading to the responsive release of NO from GSNO grafted onto ACN, which then reacts with the increased ROS to generate more toxic RNS. Collectively, the approach described herein offers substantial potential for advancing the treatment of malignant tumors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tumor penetration
Laser trigger
ROS
RNS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of NO and ONOO− generation
In vitro cell biocompatibility and cytotoxicity
In vivo fluorescence imaging and infrared thermography
Photothermal properties and RNS generation of MnO2@AI
Evaluation of in vitro therapeutic efficacy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81871368), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (CSTB2023NSCQ-MSX0747), and Foundation for Distinguished Professor of Chongqing Medical University (R13005).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of MnO2@AI. (a) TEM image of ACN, inset is size distribution; (b) HRTEM of ACN; TEM images of (c) MnO2 and (d) MnO2@AI; (e) Elemental mapping TEM images of MnO2@AI; (f) Hydrated particle size; (g) Zeta potential (n = 3); (h) The full scan XPS spectrum; Valence analysis of (i) Mn and (j) Au elements; (k) Specific surface area and (l) pore size distribution of MnO2; (m) FT-IR spectra.

ROS generation. ROS production (a) under different treatments, ICG (Control) represents a pure ICG solution without the application of the TMB method, (b) at various times, and (c) across different pH; (d) GSH consumption; (e) O2 generation (n = 3); (f) pH change (n = 3); (g) Ability of different preparations to consume glucose (n = 3); (h) Measurement of intracellular ROS; (i) Quantitative analysis of (h) (n = 3); (j) The effect of MnO2@AI on 4T1 cell activity at different glucose concentrations (n = 6); (k) Measurement of intracellular ROS at different glucose concentrations; (l) Quantitative analysis of (k) (n = 3). Data were analyzed with one-way ANOVA. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001.

Photothermal properties and RNS generation of MnO2@AI. (a) Photothermal heating capacity; (b) Photothermal conversion ratio; (c) Photothermal stability; (d) Extracellular (n = 3) and (e) intracellular NO generation; (f) Extracellular and (g) intracellular ONOO− generation; (h) Cytotoxicity of MnO2@AI (n = 6); (i) Live/Dead cell identification by FDA/PI staining. Data were analyzed with one-way ANOVA. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001.

Permeability and retention. Penetration in (a) 3D MCSs and (b) in ex vivo 4T1 tumor tissues; (c) In vivo FLI of mice after injecting ACN and MnO2@AI, respectively, the tumor is indicated by circles; (d) Quantitative fluorescence analysis of tumor sites (n = 3); (e) Fluorescence images of isolated tumors and organs; (f) Quantitative analysis of (e) (n = 3). Data were analyzed with one-way ANOVA. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001.

In vivo antitumor effects and biosafety. (a) Schematic interpretation of the treatment process for mouse breast tumor models; (b) Tumor volume change curves of mice during treatment (n = 5); (c) Tumor weight (n = 5), (d) shape, and size of mice at the end of treatment; (e) the tumor volume change curve of each animal in each group; (f) Photothermal imaging in vivo; (g) In vivo ONOO− detection; (h) Quantitative analysis of (g) (n = 3); (i) Hemolysis test (n = 6); (j) Body weight change curves of mice during treatment (n = 5); (k) Detection of various indexes of liver and kidney function (n = 3). Data were analyzed with one-way ANOVA. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001.
Schematic illustrations of (A) the design and preparation of MnO2@AI and (B) MnO2@AI for deep tumor penetration and tumor therapy.

head.ce:title: A highly permeable nanoplatform based on functionalized carbon dots for synergistic reactive oxygen/nitrogen species tumor therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Research Center for Pharmaceutical Engineering, Center for Pharmaceutical Development and Nanomedicine, Chongqing Key Laboratory of Biochemistry and Molecular Pharmacology, College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University, No. 1, Yixueyuan Road, Yuzhong District, Chongqing, 400016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Research Center for Pharmaceutical Engineering
Center for Pharmaceutical Development and Nanomedicine
Chongqing Key Laboratory of Biochemistry and Molecular Pharmacology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Research Center for Pharmaceutical Engineering, Center for Pharmaceutical Development and Nanomedicine, Chongqing Key Laboratory of Biochemistry and Molecular Pharmacology, College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University, No. 1, Yixueyuan Road, Yuzhong District, Chongqing, 400016, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS)-based antitumor strategies, particularly chemodynamic therapy, have garnered considerable attention. However, challenges such as difficulties in achieving deep penetration, relatively low H2O2 levels in the tumor microenvironment, the requirement for low pH by the Fenton reaction, and their short lifespan have impeded satisfactory therapeutic outcomes. Hence, we have developed a nanoplatform with enhanced permeability that not only generates significant amounts of ROS but also converts them into longer-lasting reactive nitrogen species (RNS), thereby improving tumor therapy efficacy. In our study, carbon dots were functionalized by doping with gold atoms and grafting nitrosoglutathione (GSNO) to form ACN, which exhibits glucose oxidase-like properties and enables laser-responsive NO release. ACN and indocyanine green (ICG) were then loaded onto MnO2 nanoflowers to form MnO2@AI. Upon arrival at the tumor site, MnO2 reacts with H2O2 and GSH, leading to its degradation and the subsequent release of ACN, which is characterized by three permeation-promoting properties: ultra-small size, positive charge, and NO content. In addition, ACN promotes H2O2 production through glucose metabolism and reduces pH, both of which enhance the Fenton-like reaction of MnO2, thereby amplifying ROS generation. The ICG in MnO2@AI enhances its photothermal properties, leading to the responsive release of NO from GSNO grafted onto ACN, which then reacts with the increased ROS to generate more toxic RNS. Collectively, the approach described herein offers substantial potential for advancing the treatment of malignant tumors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS), induced by the Fenton reagent, have long been recognized as highly reactive chemical species capable of effectively killing cancer cells [1,2]. ROS can damage biological macromolecules, including lipids, proteins, and DNA, thereby inducing apoptosis in cancer cells [3]. Manganese dioxide nanoparticles (MnO2) has recently gained significant attention for cancer diagnostic and therapeutic applications due to its sensitivity to the tumor microenvironment (TME) [4,5]. As a catalase-mimicking material, MnO2 can decompose endogenous H2O2 into O2, helping to alleviate hypoxia. Notably, MnO2 also serves as an effective glutathione (GSH)-depleting agent and releases Mn2+ ions, which can function as a chemodynamic therapy (CDT) agent. Therefore, combining MnO2 with glucose oxidase (GOx) is anticipated to create an efficient enzymatic cascade: The oxidation of glucose produces an acidic environment and abundant H2O2, which further fuels MnO2 for O2 production and enhances Mn2+-mediated Fenton-like reactions.

However, the therapeutic efficacy of ROS-based treatments is severely hampered by the inherently short lifetimes of ROS, such as 10−6 s for singlet oxygen (1O2) and 10−9 s for hydroxyl radicals (·OH) [6]. Interestingly, ROS can be transformed into reactive nitrogen species (RNS) through their reaction with nitric oxide (NO), thereby enhancing the therapeutic effect. NO, an endogenous gas, exerts various biological activities such as vasodilatation, anti-inflammation, inhibition of platelet aggregation, immune modulation [7], and anti-tumor effects [8]. At very low concentrations (<1 μM), NO promotes tumor growth, while at higher concentrations, it kills tumors by inducing DNA cleavage and toxicity to membrane proteins through oxidation or nitrosation [9]. In addition, NO can improve the accumulation of nanoparticles in tumors by vasodilating blood vessels and modulating the extracellular matrix [10,11]. However, the short half-life, limited penetration distance, and uncontrolled distribution of NO molecules limit its clinical application [12]. NO can react with ROS to produce peroxynitrite anion (ONOO−), which is more toxic and characterized by greater stability, a longer lifetime (10−2 s), and more sustained antitumor activity compared to NO and ROS [6].

In addition, to enhance the efficacy of tumor therapy, it is imperative that therapeutic agents exhibit efficient tumor penetration and retention capabilities, thereby enabling the eradication of more tumor cells. The penetration of nanoparticles into the deeper tumor parenchyma is impeded by characteristics of the TME, including high heterogeneity, a dense extracellular matrix, and high interstitial fluid pressure [13]. Throughout the penetration process, the size, shape, charge, and targeting modifications of nanoparticles influence their diffusion rate at the tumor site, thus impacting their penetration capability [14]. Size is one of the most critical factors, as ultra-small nanoparticles (typically <10 nm) enable deep tumor tissue permeability even without modification [15]. Carbon dots (CDs), a type of ultra-small carbon-based nanoparticles (<10 nm), have attracted significant interest in the field of drug delivery [16,17] owing to their favorable biocompatibility, exceptional water solubility, ease of surface functionalization, and straightforward preparation [18,19]. The size of CDs is crucial in enhancing tumor penetration. Despite the high permeability of CDs, their ultra-small size leads to faster clearance and shorter blood circulation times, posing challenges in eradicating tumor cells [15,20,21]. To reconcile this contradiction, nanoparticles with programmed changes in size have been developed [22–24]. The larger sized nanoparticles circulate in the bloodstream for a longer period of time and upon reaching the tumor, the smaller sized nanoparticles are released under the stimulation of enzyme overexpression, acidic pH, glutathione, etc., resulting in a more homogeneous distribution within the tumor and a better anti-tumor effect [25,26].

Herein, we developed functionalized CDs (ACN) by doping with gold atoms and grafting nitrosoglutathione (GSNO), which are characterized by GOx-like effects and a light-responsive release of NO (Scheme 1 A). This combination of ultra-small size, positive charge, and NO content is expected to significantly enhance the permeability of ACN to tumors. To improve its in vivo retention time, ACN was loaded onto MnO2 nanoflowers, which not only possess a large specific surface area but also serve as a Fenton reagent [4,5]. Subsequently, indocyanine green (ICG) is introduced to synthesize MnO2@AI, which endows the nanoplatform with photothermal properties [27] that facilitate the release of NO. As shown in Scheme 1B, MnO2@AI is capable of reacting with H2O2 and GSH at the tumor site, leading to the degradation and subsequent release of ACN, thereby enhancing tumor penetration. Simultaneously, ACN promotes the Fenton reaction by consuming glucose to replenish H2O2 and lower pH levels. This mechanism generates a substantial amount of ROS, which, upon interacting with NO produced following laser irradiation, form a more lethal RNS, expected to exert a more potent antitumor effect.

Citric acid, polyethyene polyamine, polyethylene glycol (PEG, average MW 10000) and ICG were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98 %), S-Nitrosoglutathione (GSNO) and N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC, 95 %) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co. (Shanghai, China). Formamide, GSH, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), 5,5′-Dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) and poly (allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH, MW18000∼20000) were sourced from Adamas-beta (Shanghai, China). Gold chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O) was provided by Sigma Aldrich (USA). Potassium permanganate (KMnO4), NaOH and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were purchased from Boyi Chemical Reagent Co. (China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dulbecco's minimum essential medium and RIMP-1640 medium was purchased from Thermo Fisher (Gibco, USA). 0.25 % trypsin EDTA and penicillin−streptomycin were obtained from Mishu Biotechnology Co. (Xi'an, China). RIMP-1640 medium without glucose and 20 % glucose solution were bought from Beijing Lanjieke Technology Co. (China). 3-[4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), Calcein/PI Live/Dead Viability/Cytotoxicity Assay Kit, Reactive Oxygen Species Assay Kit (DCFH-DA), DAF-FM diacetate and Mitochondrial membrane potential assay kit (JC-1) were obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology (China). Peroxynitrite anion (ONOO−) detection kit was bought from Biorab Technology Co. (Beijing, China).

A mixture of 20 mL polyethylene polyamine and 4.2 g citric acid was reacted at 170 °C for 4 h. The reaction mixture was then cooled to room temperature and acetone was added to induce product precipitation. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm and washed with acetone three times. The product was then dialyzed for 24 h using dialysis bags with a MWCO of 3500 Da to obtain CDs. HAuCl4 solution (200 μL, 10 mM) was added to CDs dispersion (2 mL), followed by the addition of NaOH solution to adjust the pH to 12. NaBH4 solution (400 μL, 0.5 mg/mL) was then added and the mixture was dialyzed for 24 h to obtain AuCD.

3.8 mg of EDC and 4.6 mg of NHS were added to the above AuCD dispersion and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, 10 mg of GSNO was added, and the mixture was stirred overnight in the dark. The mixture was then dialyzed for 24 h to obtain ACN.

KMnO4 (60 mg) was dissolved in ultrapure water (32 mL) and stirred until completely dissolved. Subsequently, formamide (1 mL) was added dropwise, and the reaction was conducted for 8 h at 40 °C. The resulting precipitate was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm and washed three times with ultrapure water to obtain MnO2.

The ACN prepared above and 10 mL of 1 mg/mL PAH solution were mixed with MnO2 dispersion (20 mL of 0.5 mg/mL) and stirred for 12 h. The product was collected by centrifugation. The precipitate was then dispersed in ultrapure water, and 5 mg of ICG and 10 mg of PEG were added. The mixture was stirred overnight, centrifuged, and washed to obtain MnO2@AI. All reactions were carried out in the dark. MnO2@I was prepared by replacing ACN with an equal volume of ultrapure water while keeping the rest of the steps unchanged.

The size distribution and zeta potential of various nanoparticles were measured using dynamic light scattering (DLS) via a Nano-ZEN3600 instrument (UK). The morphology of the nanoparticles was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN instrument (USA). The valence states of the major elements were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Thermo escalab 250XI instrument (USA). The concentration of Au and Mn in MnO2@AI was quantified using ICP-MS (Agilent ICPOES730, USA) after digestion with aqua regia. The specific surface area and pore size were determined using an automated specific surface area and pore size analyzer (MicrotracBEL BELSORP MINI X, Japan). The photothermal conversion performance was measured using an 808 nm near-infrared laser (LWIRL808-2W-F, China) and recorded by an infrared thermography camera (FLIR E6xt, USA). UV–vis–NIR absorption values were determined using a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The IR spectra were characterized using a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Fisher Scientific IS50 NIR, USA).

Mouse breast cancer (4T1) cells were cultured in RPMI 1640. Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) were cultured in DMEM. The addition of fetal bovine serum (10 %), penicillin (1 %, v/v), and streptomycin (1 %, v/v) was made to all mediums. The cells were cultured in a humidified incubator at an environment of 37 °C and a CO2 concentration of 5 %.

The intracellular uptake of MnO2@AI in 4T1 cells was investigated. 4T1 cells were seeded in 24-well plates (1 × 104 cells/well) and incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, cells were co-cultured with MnO2@AI for different times. After fixation with a 4 % paraformaldehyde solution, the nuclei were stained with DAPI for 20 min. Finally, the intracellular fluorescence was determined by inverted fluorescence microscopy (Olympus IX53, Japan).

The generation of ROS in solution was detected using TMB. The TMB solution containing H2O2 was mixed with MnO2@AI dispersed in PBS buffer of different pH. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used at different time points to measure the absorbance at 652 nm. The same method was used to quantify the ·OH produced by ACN, MnO2, ICG. DTNB was used to evaluate the ability of MnO2@AI to scavenge GSH. MnO2@AI dispersed in PBS buffer was mixed with GSH solution, and then DTNB solution was added. The mixture was collected at different time points, centrifuged to obtain the supernatant, and the absorbance at 412 nm was measured.

To detect intracellular ROS generation, the DCFH-DA probe was employed. Briefly, 4T1 cells were seeded on 24-well plates (1 × 104) and incubated for 24 h, and then 0.5 mL of MnO2 and MnO2@AI with or without H2O2 (100 μM) was added to each well. After 6 h of incubation, DCFH-DA was added. The fluorescent signal was detected after 1 h. ROS generated in media with varying glucose concentrations were detected using the same methodology.

A handheld dissolved oxygen meter (JPB-607A, China) was used to measure dissolved oxygen concentration. MnO2@AI (0.5 mg) containing H2O2 (1 mM) was dispersed in 5 mL of PBS buffer of different pH, and the control group was left without H2O2. Dissolved oxygen concentrations were monitored at various time points.

A glucose solution (pH 7.4) was placed in a test tube, to which MnO2@AI was added, and the mixture was then incubated in a shaker at 37 °C. The pH was measured at various times using a pH meter, conducting three parallel measurements at each time point. Glucose levels were monitored using a glucometer.

Real-time temperature monitoring was conducted using a digital thermocouple thermometer (TES-1310, China) to evaluate the photothermal performance. The temperature changes were determined after irradiation at different laser intensities, different formulations, and different concentrations with an 808 nm laser for 10 min. Furthermore, the temperature variations of free ICG, MnO2, and MnO2@AI were investigated during four laser irradiation cycles at an intensity of 1.0 W/cm2. The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) was determined using the following equation:
η =
h s
(
Δ T
m i x , max
−
Δ T
w a t e r , max
)
Ι
(
1 −
10
−
A 808
)
× 100 %
where surface area of the quartz cuvette and heat transfer coefficient are represented by s and h, respectively. The maximal temperature changes for the sample and water are denoted by ΔTmin,max and ΔTwater,max, respectively. The absorbance of the sample at 808 nm is denoted as A808. Ι symbolizes the potency of the NIR laser.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of NO and ONOO− generation
ce:section.ce:para: NO release from the solution was detected using the Griess reagent. Briefly, MnO2@AI was placed in a cuvette and irradiated with a 1 W/cm2 laser for 30 min. 50 μL of reaction solution was sampled at 5 min intervals, and NO concentration was measured. DAF-FM DA was used as a probe to observe intracellular NO production. 4T1 cells were seeded on 24-well plates (1 × 104) and incubated for 24 h. Then MnO2@AI was added, in which the MnO2@AI + NIR group was irradiated with a laser (5 min, 1 W/cm2). After 6 h of incubation, 0.2 mL of DAF-FM DA (5 μM) was added. The fluorescent signal was detected after 20 min.

ONOO− production was detected by ONOO− detection kit. Different preparations were dispersed in PBS, treated with H2O2 and laser irradiation, and then incubated with ONOO− probe at 37 °C for 20 min. The same method was used to detect intracellular ONOO−. 4T1 cells were treated with MnO2@AI containing 100 μM H2O2 for 24 h, the control was a blank medium containing 100 μM H2O2. Then, the ONOO− detection probe was added. The fluorescent signal was detected after 20 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell biocompatibility and cytotoxicity
ce:section.ce:para: HUVEC cells were seeded in 96-well plates (7 × 103 cells/well) and incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were treated with DMEM containing MnO2 at various concentrations. 24 h later, MTT was added and cultured for 4 h. Finally, the absorbance was measured at 490 nm using a microplate reader (Varioskan lux, Thermo, USA).

4T1 cells were treated with MnO2@AI, MnO2@AI + NIR, MnO2@AI + H2O2 and MnO2@AI + NIR + H2O2, respectively (+ NIR indicates irradiation with an 808 nm laser at 1 W/cm2, + H2O2 indicates the addition of 100 μM H2O2). After 24 h of culture, cell viability was assayed by the MTT assay and the Calcein-AM/PI staining. The same method was used to detect cell viability at different glucose concentrations.

The cells were treated with MnO2@I, MnO2@AI, MnO2@I + H2O2, and MnO2@AI + H2O2, respectively, followed by laser irradiation. Subsequently, the JC-1 probe was added and incubated for 20 min. Then, the cells were washed with pre-cooled 1 × staining buffer, and the fluorescence signal was detected.

Female Balb/c mice (6–7 weeks, ∼20 g) were purchased from the Experimental Animal House of Chongqing Medical University. The mice were housed in individually ventilated cages (IVC) and provided with standard chow and water ad libitum. The animal experiments were carried out in compliance with the National Guidelines for Institutional Animal Care and Chongqing Medical University Laboratory Animal Management Regulations, and were approved by the Ethics Committee of Chongqing Medical University (Approval No. 2022025).

The right hind back of the mice was injected with 50 μL of 4T1 cell suspension, which contains approximately 1 × 106 cells. The mice were euthanized when the tumor volume reached ∼400 mm3. Then, the tumor tissues were excised, cut into 1 mm3 pieces, and subcutaneously transplanted into the right hind back region of recipient mice [28].

The permeability of nanoparticles was studied in 4T1 three-dimensional multicellular spheroids (3D MCSs). The 4T1 cells were inoculated in ultra-low adsorption round-bottomed 96-well plates (3000 cells/well) and cultured for 7 days, with the medium changed every 2 days. Then 3D MCSs were incubated with ACN and MnO2 for 24 h. ACN and MnO2 were labeled with ICG in advance. Fluorescence layer scanning was performed with a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) to observe the permeability of ACN and MnO2 in 3D MCSs.

Further, permeability studies were conducted using ex vivo tumor tissue. When the tumor volume reached approximately ∼200 mm3, the tissue was excised and cultured in a medium containing ACN and MnO2. After 24 h of incubation, tumors were rinsed with PBS and then cryosectioned. Fluorescence imaging was used to visualize the cells after DAPI was used to stain the nuclei.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence imaging and infrared thermography
ce:section.ce:para: ACN and MnO2@AI were injected intravenously when the tumor volume reached ∼200 mm3. Mice were observed at indicated time points after injection using the VISQUE Smart system (Viber lourmat, France). After 24 h of injection, the mice were euthanized, and their tumors as well as vital organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) were collected for analysis.

MnO2@AI and saline were administered to tumor-bearing mice, respectively. 24 h later, the tumors were subjected to laser. The irradiation lasted for 5 min and the tumors were photographed using an infrared camera.

Female Balb/c mice bearing 4T1 subcutaneous tumor models were randomly divided into 6 groups (n = 5) when the tumor size got about ∼200 mm3: (1) saline; (2) saline + NIR; (3) MnO2; (4) ACN; (5) MnO2@AI; and (6) MnO2@AI + NIR. After 24 h of intravenous injection, a laser at 808 nm (1 W/cm2) was used to irradiate the NIR group. Body weight and tumor volume were measured daily, and the following formula was used to calculate the tumor volume: V = L × W2/2 (L is the longest dimension and W is the shortest dimension). The hearts, livers, spleens, lungs, kidneys, and tumors of each group of mice were extracted following euthanasia. The organs were sectioned, paraffin-embedded, fixated with 4 % paraformaldehyde, and stained with H&E. Before euthanasia, blood samples were collected from both the saline group and the MnO2@AI + NIR group in order to perform liver and kidney function tests.

Related data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analysis was performed using GraphPad Prism 8. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used for statistical evaluation. Statistical significance was assigned to differences with p values less than 0.05 (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001).

The detailed synthesis of MnO2@AI is shown in Scheme 1A. Ultra-small-sized CDs were first prepared, and then doped with gold atoms by reducing HAuCl4 with NaBH4, and further grafted with GSNO to obtain gold-doped and GSNO-modified ultra-small CDs (ACN). MnO2 was prepared as a carrier by reducing KMnO4 with formamide, and further loaded with ACN and ICG to obtain MnO2@AI.

The TEM image of ACN displays a size distribution of 2.7 ± 0.7 nm (Fig. 1 a). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image revealed that ACN possesses high crystallinity similar to graphene, with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1b). MnO2 nanoflowers were observed as distinct flower-like structures (Fig. 1c), and ACN was uniformly attached to the MnO2 nanoflowers surfaces (Fig. 1d). The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) results revealed the presence of Mn, O, Au, N, and C elements in MnO2@AI, where Mn predominantly originated from MnO2, and Au mainly originated from the doping of gold atoms in ACN (Fig. 1e). DLS results reveal that the average diameter of MnO2 is 100.9 ± 0.6 nm with a zeta potential of −30.6 ± 0.4 mV, increasing to 144.6 ± 1.6 nm after loading of ACN, along with a change in potential of 18.5 ± 0.8 mV. Further adsorption of ICG leads to no significant change in size and a potential of −15.9 ± 0.4 mV (Fig. 1f and g). Moreover, the elemental composition and valence states of MnO2@AI were further investigated using XPS. The XPS results confirmed the presence of Mn, O, Au, N, and C elements in MnO2@AI (Fig. 1h). Narrow scan XPS spectra exhibited two characteristic peaks corresponding to Mn (IV) 2p 1/2 (653.9 eV) and 2p 3/2 (642.4 eV), indicating the presence of MnO2 (Fig. 1i). Additionally, two characteristic peaks corresponding to elemental Au 4f 5/2 (88.4 eV) and 4f 7/2 (83.9 eV) were observed, suggesting the predominant existence of Au0 (Fig. 1j). The ICP-MS results revealed that the percentage contents of Mn and Au in MnO2@AI were 21.3 % and 1.6 %, respectively. The specific surface area and pore size of MnO2 were determined using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms revealed that MnO2 exhibited a high specific surface area of 224 m2/g and conformed to the type IV isotherm, characteristic of mesoporous materials (Fig. 1k). Pore size analysis indicated an average pore size of 10.6 nm (Fig. 1l). Meanwhile, the mean particle size of ACN was determined to be 2.7 ± 0.7 nm. These results demonstrate that MnO2 possesses a high specific surface area and suitable pore diameter, making it a promising candidate carrier for ACN. The FT-IR spectra revealed that, compared with CDs and AuCD, ACN displayed several new peaks at 1272 cm−1 and 1330 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the N-O bond [29,30], indicating the successful grafting of GSNO onto ACN (Fig. S1). Furthermore, the characteristic peak of ACN at 1650 cm−1 originates from the stretching vibration of the C=O bond in the amide group. ICG exhibited absorption peaks at 2932, 1356, and 1095 cm−1, consistent with the stretching vibrations of -NH, -CH3, and C-O, respectively [31,32]. The presence of ACN and ICG in MnO2@AI was clearly identified as expected (Fig. 1m). The stability of MnO2@AI was investigated by measuring changes in particle size and polydispersity index (PDI). The results showed no significant changes in particle size or PDI over 30 days (Fig. S2), indicating good stability. The drug loading efficiency (LE) and encapsulation efficiency (EE) of ICG were measured to be 20.6 ± 1.5 % and 98.7 ± 0.7 %, respectively.

MnO2 can decompose under acidic conditions to generate O2 and Mn2+, which catalyzes the Fenton-like reaction to produce toxic ·OH from H2O2, thereby exhibiting CDT activity [33,34]. TMB can be oxidized to oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) by ·OH, which exhibits a unique maximum absorption peak near 650 nm. The absorbance at 650 nm of different treatments was examined (Fig. 2 a), indicating that ·OH was mainly produced by MnO2 and continued to be produced over time (Fig. 2b). The production of ·OH at different pH was compared, and the results showed that more ·OH was produced at lower pH (Fig. 2c). GSH is an important antioxidant in TME that can impede tumor cell killing by ROS. The ability of MnO2@AI to deplete GSH was assessed using DTNB. The results obtained showed that the absorbance at 412 nm decreased significantly over time, demonstrating the efficient consumption of GSH by MnO2@AI (Fig. 2d). The O2 generation of MnO2@AI was measured under various conditions using a portable dissolved oxygen meter. MnO2@AI generated more O2 at pH 6.5 and 5.5 than at pH 7.4, indicating that H+ promotes O2 generation (Fig. 2e).

Importantly, MnO2@AI exhibits a GOx-like effect, attributed to the doping of gold [35,36], catalyzing the conversion of glucose into glucuronic acid, which in turn lowers the pH (Fig. 2f). This reduction in pH facilitates not only the production of O2 but also enhances the generation of ·OH. To further validate the GOx-like effect of MnO2@AI, glucose consumption of various preparations was examined using a glucometer. MnO2@AI significantly reduced glucose levels compared to the control and MnO2@I groups, suggesting a GOx-like effect attributable to ACN (Fig. 2g).

To evaluate the uptake and internalization of therapeutic agents by target cells, we examined the uptake efficiency of MnO2@AI in 4T1 cells. The internalization of MnO2@AI was monitored through the red fluorescence signal of ICG within MnO2@AI using inverted fluorescence microscopy. The results demonstrated that the red fluorescence increased with incubation time, indicating efficient uptake of MnO2@AI by the 4T1 cells (Fig. S3).

Intracellular ·OH generation was determined using DCFH-DA, which exhibited green fluorescence upon oxidation by ROS. The control group, with or without H2O2 addition, showed no green fluorescence. In contrast, MnO2 and MnO2@AI exhibited stronger green fluorescence upon H2O2 addition compared to their respective controls, indicating ·OH generation. Interestingly, MnO2@AI without H2O2 addition also exhibited green fluorescence, attributed to the GOx-like activity of ACN, which oxidized glucose in the medium to produce H2O2 and consequently ·OH. Notably, the fluorescence signal of the MnO2@AI + H2O2 group was more intense compared to the MnO2 + H2O2 group, further supporting that ACN promotes ·OH production through GOx-like effects (Fig. 2h and i). In fact, we observed that MnO2@AI differentially affected 4T1 cells at different glucose concentrations. The inhibitory effect of MnO2@AI on 4T1 cells was significantly more pronounced at high glucose concentrations compared to low glucose concentrations (Fig. 2j). We hypothesize that this phenomenon is attributable to the higher glucose concentrations, which result in elevated ·OH production. This hypothesis was confirmed by examining intracellular ·OH production at varying glucose concentrations (Fig. 2k and l). In the absence of glucose (0 μg/mL), the MnO2@AI group did not show any fluorescence signal. A weak green fluorescence emerged at a glucose concentration of 100 μg/mL. At a glucose concentration of 200 μg/mL, intense fluorescence was observed. These results indicate that the production of ·OH increased with increasing glucose concentration.
The above results indicate that ACN not only creates favorable conditions for Fenton-like reaction by lowering pH but also increases ROS production by consuming glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal properties and RNS generation of MnO2@AI
ce:section.ce:para: The photothermal properties was optimized to enhance the efficiency of NO release. The temperature of MnO2 increased by 12.6 °C, and that of MnO2@AI increased by 33 °C, indicating that ICG loading significantly enhanced the photothermal conversion capability (Fig. 3 a). Furthermore, the temperature of MnO2@AI exhibited a positive correlation with both concentration (Fig. S4) and the laser power density (Fig. S5). The MnO2@AI's photothermal conversion efficiency was calculated to be 32.0 % ± 2.0 % (Fig. 3b). Importantly, the binding of ICG with MnO2 greatly enhanced its photothermal stability (Fig. 3c and S6, 7).

Initially, the release of NO from the solution was detected using the Griess reagent. Under laser irradiation, MnO2@AI was able to sustain NO release (Fig. 3d). Furthermore, intracellular NO release was detected using the DAF-FM DA fluorescent probe. The fluorescence intensity of the MnO2@AI + NIR group was significantly higher than that of the MnO2@AI group, indicating that laser irradiation could promote the release of NO (Figs. 3e and S8). This can be attributed to the fact that the NO donor in MnO2@AI was GSNO grafted on ACN, and the -S-N=O bond in GSNO is susceptible to cleavage under light and heat conditions, resulting in the production of NO [37]. We have verified that the MnO2@AI has good photothermal properties, which contributes to the enhanced NO production upon laser irradiation.

Subsequently, both intracellular and extracellular ONOO− generation was examined. ·OH can react with H2O2 to generate O2 ·- via the Haber-Weiss reaction, and O2 ·- can further oxidize NO to form ONOO−. After various treatments (H2O2, NIR, and H2O2 + NIR) of MnO2@AI, ONOO− production was observed only with the combined H2O2 and NIR treatment (Fig. 3f). This phenomenon was attributed to the laser-controlled release of NO, which then interacted with ROS. Intracellular ONOO− production was then assayed, the green fluorescence signal was almost invisible in the control group, whereas the MnO2@AI group exhibited obvious green fluorescence, indicating the production of ONOO− (Figs. 3g and S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of in vitro therapeutic efficacy
ce:section.ce:para: The cytocompatibility of MnO2 and MnO2@AI was evaluated using HUVEC cells, and the results showed that they had good cytocompatibility (Figs. S10 and 11). Subsequently, the inhibitory effect of MnO2@AI in combination with different treatments on 4T1 cells was examined. The results revealed concentration-dependent inhibitory effects on cells in all groups. Without additional treatments, MnO2@AI alone exhibited a minimal inhibitory effect, with an inhibition rate of only 26.02 % at a concentration of 50 μg/mL. The combination of MnO2@AI with either H2O2 or NIR alone resulted in moderate growth inhibition, with inhibition rates of 46.68 % and 54.56 %, respectively. Notably, the combination of MnO2@AI with both H2O2 and NIR demonstrated a synergistic cytotoxic effect, leading to a remarkable inhibition rate of 77.7 % (Fig. 3h). Subsequently, cell apoptosis was investigated using fluorescein diacetate (FDA) and propidium iodide (PI) staining. MnO2@AI + H2O2 + NIR group exhibited the strongest red fluorescent signal, indicating the highest rate of apoptosis (Fig. 3i). These results suggest that ROS alone exhibited a weak inhibitory effect on 4T1 cells, while the combination with NIR exerted a more potent synergistic therapeutic effect, resulting in the production of the more toxic ONOO−.

Considering the potential for high oxidative stress to induce mitochondrial dysfunction, leading to metabolic exhaustion and cellular damage, mitochondrial health was assessed by measuring the mitochondrial membrane potential following JC-1 treatment. Under normal mitochondrial membrane potential conditions, JC-1 aggregates within the mitochondrial matrix, forming polymers that emit red fluorescence, indicating healthy mitochondria. However, when the mitochondrial membrane potential decreases, JC-1 remains in its monomeric form, resulting in green fluorescence, indicative of mitochondrial damage. The group treated with H2O2 exhibited stronger green fluorescence and weaker red fluorescence compared to the group without H2O2, primarily due to ROS. Importantly, the MnO2@AI + H2O2 group demonstrated a more pronounced mitochondrial damaging effect than the MnO2@I + H2O2 group, an outcome attributed to the production of RNS (Figs. S12 and 13).

3D MCSs, which mimic the microenvironment of solid tumors, are three-dimensional tumor models that exhibit tumor characteristics, such as core hypoxia and nutrient deficiencies [38,39]. A key feature of MnO2@AI is the combination of ACN with MnO2 to prolong blood circulation time and enhance tumor tissue permeability. To investigate permeability, 3D MCSs were prepared and labeled with fluorescent dyes specific to MnO2 and ACN. After incubating MnO2 and ACN with 3D MCSs for different durations, the fluorescence signal was observed by CLSM. MnO2 was primarily localized in the peripheral region of 3D MCSs and could not penetrate to the center, while ACN could permeate the core region of 3D MCSs and exhibited time dependence (Fig. 4 a). This result confirms the enhanced permeability of ACN. Further, permeability was investigated using ex vivo tumor tissue. ACN may successfully penetrate the tumor mass and reach the deeper section of the tumor, whereas MnO2 is almost exclusively located in the periphery of the tumor (Fig. 4b).

The in vivo behavior of ACN and MnO2@AI was investigated using a VISQUE Smart system. ACN and MnO2@AI were intravenously injected into tumor-bearing mice, and fluorescence images were acquired at different time points. ACN fluorescence signals decayed rapidly post-injection and failed to accumulate effectively at the tumor site. In contrast, MnO2@AI exhibited persistent high fluorescence intensity at the tumor site even at 24 h post-injection, indicating enhanced tumor enrichment and prolonged tumor retention (Fig. 4c and d). Mice were euthanized 24 h post-injection, and tumors along with major organs were harvested for fluorescence imaging. Negligible fluorescence was observed in ACN group. The MnO2@AI group exhibited strong fluorescence in tumor tissues (Fig. 4e and f). These findings demonstrated prolonged in vivo circulation of ACN upon loading onto MnO2@AI and achieved long-term retention at the tumor site.

In vivo antitumor efficacy was evaluated in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice, which were treated with various drugs for 14 days, followed by euthanasia (Fig. 5 a). The inhibitory effect on tumors was illustrated by comparing the average tumor sizes among different groups of mice (Fig. 5b,e). The tumor volume increased rapidly during treatment in saline and saline + NIR groups. The tumor growth trend was moderately slower in the ACN, MnO2, and MnO2@AI groups compared to the saline group. Notably, the MnO2@AI + NIR group exhibited significantly greater tumor inhibition than the other groups, possibly due to two reasons: (i) the ultra-small-sized ACN has a short in vivo retention time and requires simultaneous light and heat conditions to generate more NO, resulting in insufficient tumor inhibition; (ii) MnO2 as a larger carrier is not permeable enough and features only a single ROS modality, which limits its tumor inhibition efficacy. The combination of ACN, MnO2, and ICG enabled synergistic multimodal therapies, overcoming the limitations of single therapies. ACN loaded onto MnO2 prolonged retention time and slowed down its clearance rate, thus enhancing therapeutic efficacy. Additionally, the incorporation of ICG improved the photothermal properties, promoting NO release, which in turn facilitated the generation of highly cytotoxic ONOO−. At the end of treatment, tumor tissues were excised from euthanized mice, weighed (Fig. 5c), and photographed (Fig. 5d), showing that the MnO2@AI + NIR group had remarkably reduced mean tumor weight and size compared to saline group, confirming the above findings.

To investigate the in vivo generation of ONOO−, the photothermal performance of MnO2@AI was first verified in vivo. Under laser irradiation, the temperature at the tumor site in mice from the MnO2@AI group was significantly elevated (Fig. 5f), indicating that MnO2@AI retains strong photothermal properties in vivo. Then, staining of ONOO− in tumor tissues revealed that MnO2@AI + NIR group emitted distinct green fluorescence, confirming the substantial production of ONOO− (Fig. 5g and h). This result was consistent with the in vitro experiments, further confirming that the conversion of ROS to RNS contributed to a stronger antitumor effect.

The hemocompatibility of MnO2@AI was evaluated. The hemolysis rate was less than 5.0 % at MnO2@AI concentrations up to 400 μg/mL (Fig. 5i), indicating that MnO2@AI do not interact with blood components and do not cause adverse effects upon entering the bloodstream. To assess the biosafety of the different formulations in mice, body weight measurements were collected daily. There was no significant change in the body weight of mice in each group (Fig. 5j). Biochemical analysis of serum samples from mice in the saline and MnO2@AI + NIR groups revealed no significant differences in liver and kidney function indexes (ALT, AST, and BUN) between the two groups, suggesting that MnO2@AI does not impair liver and kidney functions (Fig. 5k). Furthermore, we stained animal tissues treated with MnO2@AI + NIR and compared them to saline group. The H&E results showed no significant differences in the morphology or structure of the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney tissues between the MnO2@AI + NIR group and the control group (Fig. S14). The nuclei appeared normal in morphology, the cytoplasm was homogeneous, and there was no evident cellular damage or inflammatory response, indicating that MnO2@AI did not cause significant tissue toxicity under the experimental conditions. In contrast, the tumor tissues in the MnO2@AI + NIR group exhibited obvious signs of necrosis and cell membrane rupture, demonstrating its antitumor effect. The above results indicate that the MnO2@AI prepared in this study possesses good biosafety.

We have developed a nanoplatform utilizing functionalized CDs, which integrates ultra-small CDs with larger carriers. This design not only facilitates enhanced tumor penetration but also ensures prolonged tumor retention, thereby enhancing the anti-tumor efficacy. MnO2@AI generates ROS through a Fenton-like reaction with H2O2, a process that is enhanced by the GOx-like effect of ACN, which lowers pH and supplements H2O2, thereby producing more ROS. These ROS further react with NO, released upon laser stimulation, to generate the more potent ONOO−, facilitating the transition from ROS to RNS for a stronger therapeutic effect. In vivo and in vitro experiments have demonstrated that MnO2@AI can achieve both deep tumor penetration and long-term retention, enhancing the efficiency of the ROS/RNS cascade response and promoting synergistic treatment. This approach provides a new strategy for tumor therapy.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81871368), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (CSTB2023NSCQ-MSX0747), and Foundation for Distinguished Professor of Chongqing Medical University (R13005).
